coredata.dc:title: Red, green, and blue fluorescent folate-receptor-targeting carbon dots for cervical cancer cellular and tissue imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shihao
Jiang, Jie
Yan, Yinan
Wang, Ping
Huang, Gang
Kim, Nam hoon
Lee, Joong Hee
He, Dannong
coredata.dc:description: Folate receptor targeted photo-luminescent quantum carbon dots (Fr-CDs) were successfully prepared from folic acid and phenylenediamine isomers through hydrothermal approaches. Fr-CDs were spherical particles smaller than 10 nm, and emit stable green, blue and red luminescence under ultraviolet region excitation (λex = 365 nm) with maximum emissive lengths at 530, 429, and 612 nm. And the corresponding photoluminescence quantum yield as 15.4%, 12.6% and 16.2% respectively. Up-converted photoluminescent properties in near infrared 800 nm spectral region located in green, blue and yellow region. In-vitro studies showed Fr-CDs had almost none cytotoxicity (cell viability over 80%) and high affinitive to the Hela celline highly-expressed-folate-receptor membranes, and lighted on cytoplasm as the fluorescent marker. It displayed long luminescent-stability with PL intensity above 90% in ultraviolet illuminant exposure over 24 h. In in-vivo studies, Fr-CDs were internalized and accumulated in targeted cancer tissues of cervical carcinoma and the emitting fluorescence maintains over 30 min.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Folate receptor
Bioimaging
Multicolor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro fluorescent imaging and flow cytometry study

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Plan
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the research grants from the National Key Research and Development Plan ( 2016YFA0201200 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51503153 ), the Shanghai Rising-Star Program ( 17QB1402900 ), the Shanghai Pujiang Talent Program ( 15PJ1432200 ), the Shanghai outstanding academic leaders plan ( 17XD1424200 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) to (c) HRTEM images of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs; (d) to (f) AFM images of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs.
(a) XRD and (b) FT-IR spectra of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs. (a) XPS raw data showing the carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elemental signatures. (b) Deconvoluted C 1s spectrum. (c) Deconvoluted N 1s spectrum. (d) Deconvoluted O 1s spectrum.
(a)– (c) PL spectra of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs under light of different wavelength. (Inset were pictures of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs in bright and in dark).

(a) UV–vis spectra, (b) excitation spectrum, (c) Calculated CIE coordinates from the PL spectra of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs (λex = 365 nm) and (d) Photostability curves of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs under continuous excitation of an ultraviolet light (λ = 365 nm).
The typical PL decay profiles of different samples: (a) OF-CDs, (b) MF-CDs and (c) PF-CDs, respectively.

(a) PL spectra of ethanol dispersions of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs under excitation of a near-infrared light (λ = 800 nm). (b) Calculated CIE coordinates from the PL spectra of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs (λex = 800 nm).
The PL curves of (a) OF-CDs, (b) MF-CDs and (c) PF-CDs in different pH conditions under excitation of an ultraviolet light (λ = 365 nm).
(a)– (c) In vitro cell viability of NIH-3T3 cells incubated with OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs at different concentrations for 12, 24 or 48 h.

(a) Confocal imaging of OF-CDs after co-incubated with NIH-3T3 cells and HeLa cells for 2 h; Flow cytometry test of (b) NIH-3T3 and (c) HeLa cells after incubated with OF-CDs for 2 h. (d) to (f) and (g) to (i) were the test of MF-CDs and PF-CDs in order (black arrows represented HeLa cells and white arrows represented NIH-3T3 cells).
In vivo fluorescent figure of mice after intravenous injection of PF-CDs: (a) 5 min, (b)15 min, (c) 60 min.
Scheme illustration of the designed fluorescence probe for imaging cancer cells overexpressed through FR mediated uptake.
DLS and Zeta potential of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs.
XPS elemental analysis of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs.

head.ce:title: Red, green, and blue fluorescent folate-receptor-targeting carbon dots for cervical cancer cellular and tissue imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Centre for Nanotechnology, Shanghai 200241, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Centre for Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai University of Medicine and Health Sciences, Shanghai 200093, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai University of Medicine and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advenced Materials Institute for BIN Convergence Technology, Department of BIN Convergence Technology, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Jeonbuk 54896, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advenced Materials Institute for BIN Convergence Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: D.-n. He, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Folate receptor targeted photo-luminescent quantum carbon dots (Fr-CDs) were successfully prepared from folic acid and phenylenediamine isomers through hydrothermal approaches. Fr-CDs were spherical particles smaller than 10 nm, and emit stable green, blue and red luminescence under ultraviolet region excitation (λex = 365 nm) with maximum emissive lengths at 530, 429, and 612 nm. And the corresponding photoluminescence quantum yield as 15.4%, 12.6% and 16.2% respectively. Up-converted photoluminescent properties in near infrared 800 nm spectral region located in green, blue and yellow region. In-vitro studies showed Fr-CDs had almost none cytotoxicity (cell viability over 80%) and high affinitive to the Hela celline highly-expressed-folate-receptor membranes, and lighted on cytoplasm as the fluorescent marker. It displayed long luminescent-stability with PL intensity above 90% in ultraviolet illuminant exposure over 24 h. In in-vivo studies, Fr-CDs were internalized and accumulated in targeted cancer tissues of cervical carcinoma and the emitting fluorescence maintains over 30 min.

FA-doped CDs can effectively target ovarian cancer cells and aggregate in ovarian tumor tissue in a short 15 min.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Carbonaceous nanospherical (<10 nm) particles, also named as quantum carbon dots (CDs), were novel fluorescent nanomaterial exhibiting promising prospect in biological iconographic filed, which attracted researchers' interest for its chemical stability, photo-stability, surface modification availability, cell benignity and permeability [1–3]. Compared to other organic [4], polymeric [5–8], gold [9–11] fluorescent materials applied in biomedicines, CDs was alternative to replace toxic heavy metal elements quantum dots (m-QDs) in medical therapies, avoiding serious healthy menace and environmental threats to mankind [12]. Synthetic routes were generally categorized into “Top–down” method and “bottom-up” approach including laser ablation [13], arc discharge [14], electrochemical exfoliation [15], microwave treatment [16], pyrolytic process [17] and acid oxidation [18] et al. CDs deviated in fractional size, crystalline, surface chemistry, fluorescent characteristics, colloidal stability and biocompatibility. Widely reported CDs absorb/emit in visible region with blue/green photo-luminance, and their low fluorescence quantum yield (typically <10%) was attributed to emissive traps on particle surface [19]. To enhance emissive fluorescence by atomic-level modification to intrinsic electronic and optical characteristics [20,21], heteroatoms (N, S, P and Si) doping was an efficient approach. Ye et al. doped both nitrogen and sulfur elements in CDs, and the relative PL intensity reached as high as 33.5% [22]. Particularly, nitrogen element doping was found to influence the spin density and charge distribution in carbon atomic networks, which introduced emissive energy traps for facilitating the emissive recombination of localized electron–hole pairs [23–25]. Moreover, CDs could provide comprehensive stereoscopic signals with its up-converted fluorescent properties [26], which was able to penetrated into deep tissues in photon-involved long-wavelength region emissions [13,27,28].

Physiologic metabolism and targeting functionalization design were important to guarantee carbon nano-materials' safety and reliability in medical applications. Kong et al. used CDs coordinating Al3+, and successfully applied to image the distribution of Al3+ in Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells [29]. Zero-dimensional CDs avoided other materials' mechanical stiffness resulted physical piercing trauma to live cell, and exhibited excellent cell viability. The LC50 dosage was over 5 mg mL−1, which is quite excessive (over 100×) in bio-image application of CDs [30]. As the exception of exogenous materials captured by mononuclear phagocytes in reticuloendothelial system and accumulated in organs, most of CDs were observed to be rapidly excreted through renal filtration, only with minor retention in liver and spleen. However, CDs penetrate cells and disperse in different cells, tissues, and organs, representing a challenge to optical fluorescent signal capture. To overcome this drawback, targeting cell affinitive molecules including intentionally cultivated antibody [31], encrypted DNA [32], genetically encoded peptides [33] and functional manufactured ligands [34–38], were designed to be coupled on CDs by surface chemical functionalization. Folate acid (FA) was a metabolic vitamin element easy to bind with glycosylphosphatidylinositol-linked protein highly expressed in cervical cancer cells, which extra-high expression levels were correlated with tumors syndrome in diagnosis [10,39]. Folate-modified materials like magnetic-mesoporous silica nanoparticles [40] and carbon nanotubes [39] were transported by folate receptor on cytomembrane. It engulfed hetero-material through endosome, and released the compound when cancer cells' intercellular proton pump lowed the PH environment [41,42].Up to now, folate acid molecular functionalization method was exploited as targeting drug modification strategy, which was a powerful tool in cervical cancer diagnostics [43].

In this research, folate doped fluorescent CDs (Fr-CDs) were synthesized in one-step hydrothermal method (procedure illustrated in Scheme 1 ). Through carbodiimide mediated amide bond between FA's carboxylic acid groups and CDs' amino groups, the CDs were endowed with cervical cancer cells targeting abilities. Optical properties including emission peaks, photoluminescence and optical up-conversion property were investigated in this research. CDs' selectivity to cervical cancer cells were systematically studied through in vitro and in vivo analysis.

o-phenylendiamine (o-PD), m-phenylendiamine (m-PD), p-phenylendiamine (p-PD), folic acid, Rhodamine 6G, quinine sulfate dihydrate, and Rhodamine B were purchased from Sinopharm, Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DMEM, fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-Streptomycin solution and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were purchased from Gibco Co. Ltd., USA. The Cell Counting Kit (CCK-8) was purchased from Yisheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other reagents used were all purchased from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal reaction according to the previous study [44]. CDs synthesized from o-PD,m-PD and p-PD as precursors were named as O-CDs, M-CDs and P-CDs; CDs also synthesized with additional FA as sources were called OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs. In detail, CDs were synthesized by the following procedure: o-PD, m-PD and p-PD were dissolved in ethanol and then mixed with FA aqueous solution. The mixed solution was then sealed in an autoclave and stored for 12 h at 180 °C. After the reaction, the mix solution was cooled in room temperature, and purified by silica gel column chromatography with a mixture of dichloromethane and methanol as the eluent. The obtained CDs were then precipitated and suspended in ethanol or DI water to obtain clear solutions. O-CDs, M-CDs and P-CDs were prepared through similar procedure.

Atomic force microscope (AFM) images of CDs were collected using a Nanoscope IIIa multimode (Veeco Instruments, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected on an electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL), using 200 kV accelerating voltage. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on an UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 950). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) ranging from 800 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS,) was used to analyze chemical composition using an Escalab 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were recorded on a D/MAXIIA diffract meter (Rigaku, USA) at room temperature in specular reflection mode using CuKα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA). Emission lifetime were measured on a Horiba Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer and using a 376-nm laser as an excitation source. The decay traces were fitted using bi-exponential functions Y(t) based on a non-linear least squares analysis using the following equation: Y(t) = A1exp(−t/τ1) + A2exp(−t/τ2), where A1 and A2 were the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime, belonging to τ1 and τ2 [45]. The average lifetime (τave) were calculated using equations: τave = (A1τ2 1 + A2τ2 2)/(A1τ1 + A2τ2). Zeta potential and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) were determined by laser Doppler velocimetry at 25 °C using a NanoZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, U.K). The fluorescent images were taken using an inverted fluorescence microscope (F700, Hitachi). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a fluorospectrometer (LS-55, PerkinElmer) at excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 500 nm. The quantum yield was calculated using following equation: φ =
φ R
×
I
I R
×
A r
A
×
η 2
η R 2
, where, φ and I were the quantum yield and integrated emission intensity, η and A were the refractive index and optical density. The subscript R referred to the referent fluoro-material of known quantum yield.

A CCK assay was carried out to evaluate cellular toxicity in NIH-3T3 cells. NIH-3T3 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate for 24 h to adhere. Then, CDs (5 μg mL-1 to 50 μg mL-1) were added into cells for incubation for another 12, 24 and 48 h. The relative viabilities of cells were calculated with absorption values at 490 nm. Five parallel experiments were performed under identical condition. The cytotoxicity of CDs was obtained by calculating the average of the result viabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro fluorescent imaging and flow cytometry study
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells and NIH-3T3 cell lines were used in fluorescence imaging. Both cells were cultured in DMEM with 10% FBS and 0.01% PBS at 37 °C with 5% CO2. For the in vitro imaging studies, cells were incubated in a confocal dish 24 h, and then the CDs were added to dish and incubated another 2 h under standard conditions. After completed washing with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4), the cells were imaged under confocal microscopy (TCS SP5, Leica). Flow cytometry was carried out using a BD LSR II flow cytometer. NIH-3T3 and HeLa cells were cultured in 24-well plates with density of 1 × 105 per well at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, the medium was replaced by RPMI 1640 medium containing OF-CDs, MF-CDs or PF-CDs followed by the maintenance at 37 °C in a humidified environment with 5% CO2 for another 2 h. After washing and trypsinization, the NIH-3T3 and HeLa cells were analyzed using flow cytometer the in Blue, Green and Red channel under 405 nm excitation wavelength.

6-week-old female nude mice was inoculated subcutaneously with 3 × 106 HeLa cells suspended in 0.2 mL sterile saline solution. When the tumors grew to about 100 mm3, 20 μg PF-CDs was intravenously injected through the tail vein. The dose of PF-CDs of injection was 20 mg per mouse. The fluorescent images were collected by using a Maestro™ Automated In-Vivo Imaging system (CRi Maestro™, USA) under 420 nm excitation wavelength.

The HRTEM images of the Fr-CDs were shown in Fig. 1a, b and c. MF-CDs, OF-CDs and PF-CDs were particles with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, 0.19 nm and 0.2 nm, respectively, which was very close to that for the (002) facet of graphite [46]. The lattice spacing of MF-CDs, OF-CDs and PF-CDs was similar to that of the M-CDs, O-CDs and P-CDs (Supplementary Fig. 1), which displayed that the structural crystalline properties were not obviously affected by FA molecule doping and the fluorescent fractions were from crystalline graphite-structured CDs. The AFM images of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs were shown in Fig. 1. Many quasi-spherical particles were observable on the surface of mica sheet. Through statistics, the mean size of CDs were 3.8 nm, 8.2 nm and 7.4 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d, e and f).

The results of the DLS and Zeta- potential tests were listed in Table 1 . Due to hydrophilic particles' hydration effect, the average spherical diameters were 4.4, 9.4 and 9.2 nm, which were larger than the statistical size calculated from AFM and TEM, implying that the Fr-CDs were evenly dispersed in the solution. According to Zeta-potentials, All Fr-CDs were positive charged; the strong electrostatic repulsion endowed them with excellent stability in aqueous solutions. Consequently, the Fr-CDs maintained stable for long time and avoid fluorescent loss, which were helpful in storage.

The XRD curves of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs were shown in Fig. 2a. A obvious peak appeared near 20° on Fr-CDs curves (19.7°, 19.8° and 20.1° respectively). According to the Scherrer equation, the grain sizes were 0.214, 0.223 and 0.233 nm, which was close to 0.21 nm, corresponding to the 002 lattice planes of sp2graphitic carbon, also consistent with the TEM observations. The chemical functionality was characterized by FT-IR, and the curves of o-PD, m-PD, p-PD, FA, OF-CDs, MF-CDs, PF-CDs were overlapped for comparison, shown in Fig. 2b. In the FTIR spectrum of FA, the peak appeared at 1023 cm−1 indicated anhydride CO stretching, whereas the peak at 1641 cm−1 indicated an alkane CH3 bend and an aromatic CC stretch. The band at 3470 cm−1 depicted an alcoholic OH stretch and carboxylic acid OH stretch from the aqueous suspension [24,47]. Peaks about 3412 and 3342 cm−1, 1524 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1 were shown in the FTIR spectrum of PD, belonging to stretching vibrations for NH, CC and CN bonds [48]. The Fr-CDs exhibited similar IR spectra to the starting material. Notably, some new characteristic peaks appeared at approximately 3200–3320 cm−1, 1336–1384 cm−1 and 1225–1234 cm−1 and could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of aliphatic CH, CN, and CO bonds; the peaks at approximately 1690–1730 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of anhydride CO bonds, which implied that decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions occurred during the reaction [24,44,49]. The peaks around 1660 cm−1 and 1413 cm−1 represented the chemical reactive bonds that amide NH bending as well as aldehyde/anhydride CO stretching [24].

The elemental composition was identified through XPS analysis shown in Fig. 3a. The XPS spectra revealed OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs had elementary compositions including carbon (binding energy approximately as 285 eV), nitrogen (398.5 eV), and oxygen (531 eV) [44]. The carbon, nitrogen and oxygen contents of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs were 77.7, 12.2 and 10.1%; 73.9, 16.3 and 9.8%; 80.3, 10.5 and 9.2%, respectively. All Fr-CDs were carbon-rich and doped with an abundance of nitrogen atoms. High-resolution C1s XPS spectrums were shown in Fig. 3b, c and d: four peaks were deconvoluted with binding energies of approximately 284.3 eV, 285.1 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.1 eV, which were attributed to C–C/CC, CN, CO (epoxy and alkoxy) and CO/CN species, respectively [44].

The N 1s spectrum in Supplementary Fig. 2a to c had three deconvoluted peaks at approximately 398.5 eV, 399.3 eV and 400.2 eV, which were attributed to: amide/amine, pyrrolic and graphitic contained nitrogen elements [50]. The O 1s spectra in Supplementary Fig. 2d to f had two peaks at approximately 531.3 eV and 532.4 eV, which were assigned to CO and COH/COC groups [51]. The content of various groups in CDs were listed in Table 2 . The results indicated that carbon in the CDs mainly consists of graphite-structured carbon, amino-N and the hydroxyl oxygen elements.

MF-CDs, OF-CDs and PF-CDs exhibited clear brilliant yellow, canary, and ruby colours by visual observation (inset picture in Fig. 4 ), however they emitted blue, green and red fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation. Fr-CDs' emission peaks showed bathochromic effect as the excitation wavelength increasing due to excitation dependence, shifting from 528 to 535 nm, 408 to 519 nm and 602 to 624 nm respectively(Fig. 4a to c). When λex = 360 nm, Fr-CDs' quntum Yields (QYs) were determined to be 12.2, 13.1, and 10.1%. By optimizing the excitation wavelengths according to the excitation spectra (Fig. 5b), QYs reached 15.4%, 16.2% and 12.7% for OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs, respectively, which showed CDs' intensity dependence on excitation wavelength. The excitation-wavelength dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity was attributed to the different surface states including functional groups, emissive traps and edge defects, which affected the band gap of CDs [51].Thus, hetero-elements can partly affect CDs' optical properties. According to the XPS result, Fr-CDs's QYs were consistent to their nitrogen contents.

The ultraviolet-visible (UV/Vis) absorption spectra were shown in Fig. 5a. Two prominent peaks appeared in the phenylendiamine's UV/Vis spectras (m-PD at 212 and 295 nm, o-PD at 209 and 294 nm, and p-PD at 243 and 309 nm). As for the spectas of Fr-CDs, intense peaks at 280 nm, 279 nm and 285 nm were ascribed to π–π* transitions in the aromatic CC bonds [12,52], and weak peak at 420 nm, 342 nm and 405 nm were ascribed to the n–π* transitions [53]. The absorption peaks' red-shift demonstrated that smaller electronic band gaps were formed during the polymerization and carbonization process. Excitation spectra were shown in Fig. 5b, MF-CDs' peak appeared at 332 nm in UV region, but the peaks of OF-CDs and MF-CDs appeared at 450 and 513 nm in visual region.

To study the chromaticity of Fr-CDs, colorimetric measurement were carried out by using Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) color model. The CIE coordinates of OF-CDs, MF-CDs, and PF-CDs (λex = 360 nm) were (0.29, 0.63), (0.15, 0.09) and (0.66, 0.34), which were located in green, blue and red region respectively, consistent to the fluorescent image (Fig. 5c). CDs' fluorescence property was highly stable, and their PL intensity slightly (<20%) decayed under continuous illumination with a λ = 365 nm UV light for one day, which was beneficial to long-term fluorescence imaging (Fig. 5d).

Photoluminescence lifetimes were also tested by time-resolved measurements. As seen in Fig. 6a to c, the decay traces were fitted to bi-exponential functions Y(t). The bi-exponential behaviour of the lifetime suggested that two different emissive sites were present, which implied the fluorescence state indicated the graphite centre (or conjugated structures) and surface traps. According to the equation of τave, Fr-CDs' average fluorescent lifetime were calculated to be 5.9, 6.68 and 4.96 ns, comparable to other reported values [54], which revealed the radiative recombination nature of excitations. The average fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs being in the nanosecond regime proved that Fr-CDs remained in its excited state prior to returning to its ground state, which suggested that Fr-CDs resisted to photobleaching and were suitable for optoelectronic and biological applications [55,56].

The UCPL emissions were also investigated. Fr-CDs emitted bright florescence under a λ = 800 nm pulsed laser. Emission spectra in Fig. 7a illustrated that emission peak in the green light region. As calculated, CIE coordinates of OF-CDs, MF-CDs, and PF-CDs were (0.40, 0.58), (0.16, 0.33) and (0.45, 0.45), which located in green, blue and yellow region respectively (Fig. 7b).
The tumor's micro-environmental PH varied especially in the region of 6 to 8.5, and the emission spectra of Fr-CDs with different pH levels were investigated and shown in Fig. 8 .

The results showed that, under acid conditions, emission peaks of Fr-CDs varied slightly, and the PL intensity had an increasing tendency as the pH decreasing. In comparison, under alkaline conditions, the emission peaks of Fr-CDs had a redshift as the pH increasing and the PL intensity decreased rapidly to almost one-third as pH increasing to 13. But Fr-CDs' emission peaks and PL intensity didn't change obviously in the pH range from 5 to 9. And the changes in optical properties of the CDs were recoverable in the range of 3 to 12.

These phenomena indicated change of the CDs surface charges. On the surface of the Fr-CDs, different groups, including amino and carboxyl, had protonation or protonation-driven effects as the pH changed [20]. When changing back the pH from low to high, i.e., from 12 to 2, the hydroxyl generated ionized groups which neutralized the Fr-CDs surface charge and formed narrow band gap caused by deprotonation and subsequently the π-π* or n-π* transition in Fr-CDs [57], resulting in PL intensity loss.

The existence of different groups on the Fr-CDs eliminated the effect of the low content of hydrogen ions and hydroxyl through buffer function, so the Fr-CDs can maintain the majority of their PL intensity in the pH range from 6 to 8.5 [58]. In summary, the Fr-CDs we synthesized were suitable for bioimaging in most situations including tumor bioimaging.

The results of in vitro cytotoxicity were shown in Fig. 9 . The viability of NIH-3T3 cells maintained over 90% after 48 h incubation with OF-CDs' concentrations up to 10 μg mL−1. As the concentrations of OF-CDs increasing, when the concentrations were 40 μg/mL−1, there was still a minor reduction (>88%) in the viability of NIH-3T3 cells after 12 h incubation; over 75% cell viability was observed after incubation of NIH-3T3 cells with OF-CDs for 48 h. The result of MF-CDs and PF-CDs were similar to OF-CDs, thus conforming none cytotoxicity of as-prepared Fr-CDs [26].

Since all Fr-CDs demonstrated superior optical properties and good biocompatibility for multiplexed bioimaging reagents, their cellular imaging capability was preliminarily investigated to reveal potential applications of these Fr-CDs in multiplexed bioimaging. The fluorescence images were taken with a 405 nm UV light and flow cytometry of NIH3T3 and HeLa cells co-incubated with Fr-CDs at concentrations of 10 μg mL-1 in PBS buffer for 2 h were illustrated in Fig. 10 . The Fr-CDs in irregular HeLa cells (black arrow) obviously emitted stronger fluorescence compared to the Fr-CDs in fusiform NIH-3T3 cells (white arrow) (Fig. 10a, d and g), which implied that OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs can enter HeLa cells more rapidly than NIH-3T3 cells (HeLa and NIH3T3 cells can be identified by their bright photos in Supplementary Fig. 3a and b). To quantitatively analyze the situation where CDs enter either HeLa or NIH-3T3 cells, flow cytometry images of HeLa (Fig. 10b, e and h) and NIH-3T3 cells (Fig. 10c, f and i) were shown. The results illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs entering in HeLa cells (190.54 a.u., 199.52 a.u., 251.28 a.u.) were 3-fold stronger than those entering NIH-3T3 cells(61.66 a.u., 69.18 a.u., 72.28 a.u.), which were in good accordance with the results of confocal imaging, indicating greatly improved cellular uptake of Fr-CDs in HeLa cells.

Compared to NIH-3T3 cells internalizing Fr-CDs through endocytosis, HeLa cells can internalize Fr-CDs through both endocytosis and FR mediated endocytosis with nonimmunogenicity, which improved the aggregation of Fr-CDs in FR high-affinity tumor cells like HeLa cells [59,60].

We chose PF-CDs to study in vivo imaging because of its long emission peak which benefited to tissue imaging. Fig. 11 was the in vivo fluorescent images of PF-CDs in mice with HeLa tumor. The solutions was injected into mice' body through intravenous injection, and no obvious fluorescence was found in 5 min (Fig. 11a). After 15 min of circulation, strong fluorescence was observed in the tumors (Fig. 11b), indicating that the PF-CDs could be rapidly internalized by HeLa cells. The fluorescence was maintained for approximately 40 min. After injection for 1 h (Fig. 11c), the fluorescence in the tumor tissue was almost invisible. Thus, the PF-CDs still had high selectivity for HeLa cells in vivo and can be maintained in the tumor tissue for a considerable amount of time.

Novel cervical cancer targetting cellular and tissue imaging Fr-CDs were successfully synthesized through one-spot hydrothermal method using phenylenediamine and folic acid as precursors. Fr-CDs emitted Multi-color (green, blue and red) fluorescent under 365 nm wavelength excitations, and possessing up-conversion imaging ability with excellent photo-stability. The ultra-violet excited photo-luminescent Quantum Yield of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs reached 15.4, 16.2 and 12.7%, while the near-infrared excited photo-luminescent of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs located in green, blue and yellow region, respectively. Moreover, Fr-CDs showed slight bio-toxicity after incubation with NIH-3T3 cells for 48 h (<20%). Confocal microscopy and quantitative analyses demonstrated that the Fr-CDs effectively target FA high-affinity cervical cancer cells. In-vivo imaging also proved that Fr-CDs can efficiently aggregate in tumor tissue in short 15 min and stable effect as displaying agents for >40 min.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the research grants from the National Key Research and Development Plan (2016YFA0201200), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51503153), the Shanghai Rising-Star Program (17QB1402900), the Shanghai Pujiang Talent Program (15PJ1432200), the Shanghai outstanding academic leaders plan (17XD1424200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM images of C1, C2 and C3. Deconvoluted N 1s spectrum and O 1s spectrum of OF-CDs, MF-CDs and PF-CDs. This material is available free of charge via the Internet at http://pubs.acs.org. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.08.058.
